questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $0.2 \text{ kg}$ is suspended from the ceiling by a light string. A second block of mass $0.3 \text{ kg}$ is suspended from the first block through another string. Determine the tensions in the upper and lower strings, respectively. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let $m_1 = 0.2 \text{ kg}$ be the mass of the upper block and $m_2 = 0.3 \text{ kg}$ be the mass of the lower block.

For the lower block, the forces acting on it are its weight downwards and the tension $T_2$ of the lower string upwards. Since the system is in equilibrium:

$T_2 = m_2 g$

$T_2 = 0.3 \times 10 = 3 \text{ N}$

For the upper block, the forces acting on it are its weight downwards, the tension $T_2$ downwards, and the tension $T_1$ of the upper string upwards. Since it is in equilibrium:

$T_1 = m_1 g + T_2$

$T_1 = 0.2 \times 10 + 3 = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ N}$

Alternatively, the upper string supports the total mass of both blocks. Thus, the tension in the upper string is:

$T_1 = (m_1 + m_2)g = (0.2 + 0.3) \times 10 = 5 \text{ N}$

The tensions in the upper and lower strings are $5 \text{ N}$ and $3 \text{ N}$, respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $50 \text{ g}$ travels along a straight line. The variation of its speed with time is displayed in the figure. Determine the force acting on the particle at $t = 2$, $4$, and $6$ seconds.
options: $0.25 \text{ N}$ along the motion, zero, and $0.25 \text{ N}$ opposite to the motion
$0.50 \text{ N}$ along the motion, zero, and $0.50 \text{ N}$ opposite to the motion
$0.25 \text{ N}$ opposite to the motion, zero, and $0.25 \text{ N}$ along the motion
$250 \text{ N}$ along the motion, zero, and $250 \text{ N}$ opposite to the motion
questions.solution:
The mass of the particle is given as $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$.

The acceleration $a$ of the particle is the slope of the velocity-time graph, and the force is given by Newton's second law, $F = ma$.

At $t = 2 \text{ s}$, the particle is in the time interval $0$ to $3 \text{ s}$. The slope of the $v-t$ graph is:
$a = \dfrac{15 - 0}{3 - 0} = 5 \text{ m/s}^2$
The force is $F = 0.05 \times 5 = 0.25 \text{ N}$. Since the acceleration is positive, the force is along the direction of motion.

At $t = 4 \text{ s}$, the particle is in the time interval $3$ to $5 \text{ s}$. The velocity is constant, so the slope is zero:
$a = 0$
The force is $F = 0.05 \times 0 = 0 \text{ N}$.

At $t = 6 \text{ s}$, the particle is in the time interval $5$ to $8 \text{ s}$. The slope of the $v-t$ graph is:
$a = \dfrac{0 - 15}{8 - 5} = -5 \text{ m/s}^2$
The force is $F = 0.05 \times (-5) = -0.25 \text{ N}$. The negative sign indicates that the force is acting opposite to the direction of motion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Raindrops, each having a radius of $1 \text{ mm}$ and a mass of $4 \text{ mg}$, are falling at a speed of $30 \text{ m/s}$ onto the head of a bald person. Upon splashing on the head, the drops come to rest. Assuming equivalently that the drops travel a distance equal to their radii on the head during the impact, determine the force exerted by an individual drop on the head.
questions.solution:
Given, mass of a raindrop, $m = 4 \text{ mg} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}$

Initial speed of the raindrop, $u = 30 \text{ m/s}$

Final speed of the raindrop, $v = 0 \text{ m/s}$

Distance traveled during impact, $s = r = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

Using the work-energy theorem, the work done by the stopping force equals the change in kinetic energy of the raindrop:

$F \times s = \dfrac{1}{2} m u^2$

$F \times 10^{-3} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times (30)^2$

$F \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 900$

$F \times 10^{-3} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$

$F = 1.8 \text{ N}$

By Newton's third law, the force exerted by the drop on the head is equal in magnitude to the stopping force, which is $1.8 \text{ N}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a TV picture tube, electrons are emitted from the cathode with negligible speed and attain a velocity of $5 \times 10^{6} \text{ m/s}$ after travelling one centimeter. Assuming straight line motion, determine the constant force acting on the electron. The mass of the electron is $9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$.
questions.solution:
Given initial velocity $u = 0$, final velocity $v = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m/s}$, distance $s = 1 \text{ cm} = 10^{-2} \text{ m}$, and mass $m = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$.

Using the equation of motion $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$:

$a = \dfrac{v^2 - u^2}{2s}$

$a = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^6)^2 - 0}{2 \times 10^{-2}}$

$a = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{12}}{2 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.25 \times 10^{15} \text{ m/s}^2$

The constant force acting on the electron is given by Newton's second law, $F = ma$:

$F = (9.1 \times 10^{-31}) \times (1.25 \times 10^{15})$

$F = 11.375 \times 10^{-16} \text{ N}$

$F \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-15} \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block with a mass of $2 \text{ kg}$ rests on a long frictionless horizontal table and is pulled horizontally by a constant force $F$. The block is observed to travel $10 \text{ m}$ during the first two seconds. Determine the magnitude of $F$.
questions.solution:
Given initial velocity $u = 0$, distance $s = 10 \text{ m}$, and time $t = 2 \text{ s}$.

Using the equation of motion $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$:

$10 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}a(2)^2$

$10 = 2a \Rightarrow a = 5 \text{ m/s}^2$

The force $F$ is given by Newton's second law:

$F = ma$

$F = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Placed on a smooth horizontal table, two blocks of equal mass $m$ are connected by a light string. A constant force $F$ is applied to pull one of the blocks along the line connecting them. Determine the tension in the string joining the blocks.
questions.solution:
Let the acceleration of the system be $a$.

The total mass of the two blocks is $m + m = 2m$.

Since a constant force $F$ is applied to the system, the acceleration is given by Newton's second law:

$a = \dfrac{F}{2m}$

Let $T$ be the tension in the string. The only horizontal force acting on the trailing block of mass $m$ is the tension $T$.

The equation of motion for the trailing block is:

$T = ma$

Substituting the value of $a$ into the equation:

$T = m \left(\dfrac{F}{2m}\right) = \dfrac{F}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Travelling at $40 \text{ km/h}$, a car must be brought to a halt by applying brakes within the next $4.0 \text{ m}$. Given that the car weighs $2000 \text{ kg}$, determine the average force that must be applied to it.
questions.solution:
Initial velocity, $u = 40 \text{ km/h} = 40 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s} = \dfrac{100}{9} \text{ m/s}$

Final velocity, $v = 0$

Distance, $s = 4.0 \text{ m}$

Mass, $m = 2000 \text{ kg}$

Using the third equation of motion:

$v^2 - u^2 = 2as$

$0 - \left(\dfrac{100}{9}\right)^2 = 2a(4.0)$

$a = -\dfrac{10000}{81 \times 8} = -\dfrac{1250}{81} \text{ m/s}^2$

Average retarding force is given by $F = m|a|$:

$F = 2000 \times \dfrac{1250}{81} = \dfrac{2500000}{81} \text{ N}$

$F \approx 30864 \text{ N} \approx 3.1 \times 10^4 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force $F = -kx$, where $k = 15 \text{ N/m}$, acts on a particle of mass $0.3 \text{ kg}$. Determine the initial acceleration of the particle when it is released from the position $x = 20 \text{ cm}$.
questions.solution:
Given $k = 15 \text{ N/m}$, $m = 0.3 \text{ kg}$, and $x = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$.

The magnitude of the restoring force acting on the particle is given by $|F| = kx$.

Substituting the given values:

$|F| = 15 \times 0.2 = 3 \text{ N}$

The initial acceleration of the particle is $a = \dfrac{|F|}{m}$.

$a = \dfrac{3}{0.3} = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the arrangement depicted in the figure, both springs are unstretched. If the block is given a displacement of $x$ and released, what will be its initial acceleration?
options: $\dfrac{k_1 k_2 x}{(k_1 + k_2)m}$ opposite to the displacement
questions.solution:
When the block is displaced by a distance $x$ from its equilibrium position, one spring gets stretched by $x$ and the other gets compressed by $x$.

The restoring force from the first spring is $F_1 = k_1 x$ in the direction opposite to the displacement.

The restoring force from the second spring is $F_2 = k_2 x$, also in the direction opposite to the displacement.

The net restoring force acting on the block is:
$F_{net} = F_1 + F_2 = k_1 x + k_2 x = (k_1 + k_2)x$

This net force acts opposite to the direction of displacement.

Using Newton's second law, the initial acceleration $a$ of the block is:
$a = \dfrac{F_{net}}{m} = \dfrac{(k_1 + k_2)x}{m}$

The direction of the acceleration is opposite to the displacement.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small block $B$ is positioned on another block $A$ of mass $5\text{ kg}$ and length $20\text{ cm}$. Initially, block $B$ is situated near the right end of block $A$, as shown in the figure. A constant horizontal force of $10\text{ N}$ is applied to block $A$. Assuming all surfaces to be frictionless, calculate the time elapsed before block $B$ separates from block $A$.
questions.solution:
Since all surfaces are frictionless, there is no horizontal force acting on block $B$. Therefore, the acceleration of block $B$ is zero ($a_B = 0$).

The horizontal force $F = 10\text{ N}$ acts only on block $A$. The acceleration of block $A$ is given by:
$a_A = \dfrac{F}{m_A} = \dfrac{10}{5} = 2\text{ m/s}^2$

The relative acceleration of block $B$ with respect to block $A$ is:
$a_{rel} = |a_B - a_A| = |0 - 2| = 2\text{ m/s}^2$

Block $B$ is initially at the right end of block $A$. To separate from block $A$, it must cover a relative distance equal to the length of block $A$:
$s_{rel} = 20\text{ cm} = 0.2\text{ m}$

Using the second equation of motion for relative motion ($u_{rel} = 0$):
$s_{rel} = \dfrac{1}{2} a_{rel} t^2$

Substituting the values:
$0.2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 2 \times t^2$

$t^2 = 0.2$

$t = \sqrt{0.2} \approx 0.447\text{ s}$

Rounding to two decimal places, the time elapsed is $0.45\text{ s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pendulum bob of mass $50 \text{ g}$ hangs from the ceiling of an elevator. Determine the tension in the string given that the elevator is travelling upward with a deceleration of $1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Mass of the bob, $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$

The elevator is travelling upward with a deceleration of $1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$. This implies the acceleration of the elevator is directed downward, with magnitude $a = 1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Writing the equation of motion for the bob:

$mg - T = ma$

$T = m(g - a)$

Substituting $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $a = 1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$T = 0.05(9.8 - 1.2)$

$T = 0.05 \times 8.6$

$T = 0.43 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pendulum bob of mass $50 \text{ g}$ hangs from the ceiling of an elevator. Determine the tension in the string given that the elevator is travelling upward with a uniform velocity.
questions.solution:
Mass of the bob, $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$

Since the elevator is moving upward with a uniform velocity, its acceleration is zero, $a = 0$.

The forces acting on the bob are the tension $T$ in the upward direction and the weight $mg$ in the downward direction.

Applying Newton's second law of motion:

$T - mg = ma$

$T - mg = 0$

$T = mg$

Taking $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$T = 0.05 \times 9.8 = 0.49 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pendulum bob of mass $50 \text{ g}$ hangs from the ceiling of an elevator. Determine the tension in the string given that the elevator is travelling downward with an acceleration of $1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Given mass of the bob, $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$

Acceleration of the elevator, $a = 1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$ downward

Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$

When the elevator accelerates downward, the equation of motion for the bob is:

$mg - T = ma$

$T = m(g - a)$

Substituting the given values:

$T = 0.05(9.8 - 1.2)$

$T = 0.05 \times 8.6 = 0.43 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pendulum bob of mass $50 \text{ g}$ hangs from the ceiling of an elevator. Determine the tension in the string given that the elevator is travelling downward with a deceleration of $1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Mass of the bob, $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$

The elevator is travelling downward with a deceleration, which means its acceleration is directed upwards. Let this acceleration be $a = 1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Using Newton's second law with respect to the ground frame, the forces acting on the bob are the tension $T$ upwards and the weight $mg$ downwards.

The equation of motion is:

$T - mg = ma$

$T = m(g + a)$

Taking $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$, we get:

$T = 0.05 \times (9.8 + 1.2)$

$T = 0.05 \times 11.0$

$T = 0.55 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pendulum bob of mass $50 \text{ g}$ hangs from the ceiling of an elevator. Determine the tension in the string given that the elevator is travelling downward with a uniform velocity.
questions.solution:
Mass of the bob, $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$

Since the elevator is moving downward with a uniform velocity, its acceleration $a = 0$.

The forces acting on the bob are the tension $T$ in the upward direction and the weight $mg$ in the downward direction.

Balancing the forces, we get:

$T = mg$

$T = 0.05 \times 9.8$

$T = 0.49 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person stands on a weighing machine placed on the floor of an elevator. The elevator starts moving upwards with a certain acceleration, proceeds with a uniform velocity for a while, and finally decelerates to a stop. The maximum and minimum weights registered are $72 \text{ kg}$ and $60 \text{ kg}$. Given that the magnitudes of the acceleration and the deceleration are identical, determine the true weight of the person. Take $g = 9.9 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let $m$ be the true mass of the person and $a$ be the magnitude of acceleration and deceleration.

When the elevator accelerates upwards, the apparent weight is maximum. The reading on the weighing machine is given by:

$m_{max} = \dfrac{m(g + a)}{g} = m\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right) = 72$

When the elevator decelerates upwards, the apparent weight is minimum. The reading on the weighing machine is given by:

$m_{min} = \dfrac{m(g - a)}{g} = m\left(1 - \dfrac{a}{g}\right) = 60$

Adding the two equations, we get:

$m\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right) + m\left(1 - \dfrac{a}{g}\right) = 72 + 60$

$2m = 132$

$m = 66 \text{ kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person stands on a weighing machine placed on the floor of an elevator. The elevator starts moving upwards with a certain acceleration, proceeds with a uniform velocity for a while, and finally decelerates to a stop. The maximum and minimum weights registered are $72 \text{ kg}$ and $60 \text{ kg}$. Given that the magnitudes of the acceleration and the deceleration are identical, determine the magnitude of the acceleration. Take $g = 9.9 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the true mass of the person be $m$.

When the elevator accelerates upwards with acceleration $a$, the apparent weight is maximum.

$W_{max} = m(g + a)$

When the elevator decelerates upwards with acceleration $a$, the apparent weight is minimum.

$W_{min} = m(g - a)$

Taking the ratio of the maximum and minimum apparent weights:

$\dfrac{W_{max}}{W_{min}} = \dfrac{m(g + a)}{m(g - a)} = \dfrac{72}{60}$

$\dfrac{g + a}{g - a} = \dfrac{6}{5}$

$5g + 5a = 6g - 6a$

$11a = g$

$a = \dfrac{g}{11}$

Substituting $g = 9.9 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$a = \dfrac{9.9}{11} = 0.9 \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two blocks $A$ and $B$ having masses $m_A$ and $m_B$ respectively are placed in contact on a frictionless table. An experimenter pushes block $A$ from behind, causing the blocks to accelerate. If the force exerted by block $A$ on block $B$ is $F$, determine the force applied by the experimenter on block $A$.
questions.solution:
Let the force applied by the experimenter be $P$.

The blocks $A$ and $B$ move together with a common acceleration $a$.

The equation of motion for block $B$ is:

$F = m_B a \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{F}{m_B}$

The equation of motion for the system of blocks $A$ and $B$ is:

$P = (m_A + m_B) a$

Substituting the value of $a$ we get:

$P = (m_A + m_B) \left(\dfrac{F}{m_B}\right)$

$P = F \left(1 + \dfrac{m_A}{m_B}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $2 \text{ kg}$ block is suspended from the ceiling using a massless spring having a spring constant $k = 100 \text{ N/m}$. Find the elongation of the spring. If an additional $1 \text{ kg}$ is attached to the block, what would be the further elongation?
questions.solution:
Given $m_1 = 2 \text{ kg}$, $k = 100 \text{ N/m}$, and taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.

At equilibrium, the restoring force of the spring balances the weight of the block.

$k x_1 = m_1 g$

$100 x_1 = 2 \times 10$

$x_1 = 0.2 \text{ m}$

When an additional mass $m_2 = 1 \text{ kg}$ is attached, the additional restoring force balances the additional weight.

$k x_2 = m_2 g$

$100 x_2 = 1 \times 10$

$x_2 = 0.1 \text{ m}$

The initial elongation is $0.2 \text{ m}$ and the further elongation is $0.1 \text{ m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When placed deep in water, an empty plastic box of mass $m$ accelerates upward at a rate of $g/6$. Determine the mass of sand that must be added to the box so that it accelerates downward at a rate of $g/6$.
questions.solution:
Let the buoyant force on the completely submerged box be $B$.

When the empty box of mass $m$ accelerates upward at $\dfrac{g}{6}$, the equation of motion is:

$B - mg = m\left(\dfrac{g}{6}\right)$

$B = mg + \dfrac{mg}{6} = \dfrac{7mg}{6}$

Let $M$ be the mass of the sand added. The total mass becomes $m + M$. Since the volume of the box remains the same, the buoyant force $B$ remains unchanged.

For the box to accelerate downward at $\dfrac{g}{6}$, the equation of motion is:

$(m + M)g - B = (m + M)\left(\dfrac{g}{6}\right)$

Substituting the value of $B$ into the equation:

$(m + M)g - \dfrac{7mg}{6} = (m + M)\left(\dfrac{g}{6}\right)$

$(m + M)\left(g - \dfrac{g}{6}\right) = \dfrac{7mg}{6}$

$(m + M)\left(\dfrac{5g}{6}\right) = \dfrac{7mg}{6}$

$5(m + M) = 7m$

$5m + 5M = 7m$

$5M = 2m$

$M = \dfrac{2m}{5}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $2 \text{ kg}$ block is suspended from the ceiling of an elevator using a massless spring having a spring constant $k = 100 \text{ N/m}$. The elevator is going up with an acceleration of $2.0 \text{ m/s}^2$. Find the elongation of the spring. If an additional $1 \text{ kg}$ is attached to the block, what would be the further elongation?
questions.solution:
Given $m_1 = 2 \text{ kg}$, $k = 100 \text{ N/m}$, elevator upward acceleration $a = 2.0 \text{ m/s}^2$, and taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Inside the accelerating elevator, the effective acceleration due to gravity is $g_{\text{eff}} = g + a = 10 + 2.0 = 12 \text{ m/s}^2$.

At equilibrium, the restoring force balances the apparent weight of the block.

$k x_1 = m_1 g_{\text{eff}}$

$100 x_1 = 2 \times 12$

$x_1 = 0.24 \text{ m}$

When an additional mass $m_2 = 1 \text{ kg}$ is attached, the additional restoring force balances the additional apparent weight.

$k x_2 = m_2 g_{\text{eff}}$

$100 x_2 = 1 \times 12$

$x_2 = 0.12 \text{ m}$

The initial elongation is $0.24 \text{ m}$ and the further elongation is $0.12 \text{ m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A constant upward buoyancy force $B$ is exerted by the atmosphere on a balloon. The air resistance acting on the balloon is proportional to its velocity and opposes its motion. Carrying a mass $M$, the balloon is observed to fall near the earth's surface at a constant velocity $v$. Determine the amount of mass that must be removed from the balloon for it to rise at a constant velocity $v$.
questions.solution:
Let the air resistance be $kv$.

When the balloon falls with a constant velocity $v$, the air resistance acts upwards. Equating the forces for zero net force:

$Mg = B + kv$

$kv = Mg - B$

Let $M'$ be the new mass of the balloon when it rises with the same constant speed $v$. The air resistance now acts downwards. Equating the forces:

$B = M'g + kv$

Substituting the value of $kv$ from the first equation:

$B = M'g + (Mg - B)$

$M'g = 2B - Mg$

$M' = \dfrac{2B}{g} - M$

The amount of mass that must be removed is:

$\Delta M = M - M'$

$\Delta M = M - \left(\dfrac{2B}{g} - M\right)$

$\Delta M = 2M - \dfrac{2B}{g} = 2\left(M - \dfrac{B}{g}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pendulum bob of mass $50 \text{ g}$ hangs from the ceiling of an elevator. Determine the tension in the string given that the elevator is travelling upward with an acceleration of $1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Given, mass of the bob, $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$

Acceleration of the elevator, $a = 1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$ (upwards)

Taking acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$

The equation of motion for the bob is:

$T - mg = ma$

$\Rightarrow T = m(g + a)$

Substituting the values:

$T = 0.05(9.8 + 1.2)$

$T = 0.05 \times 11.0$

$T = 0.55 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, an elevator is descending with an acceleration of $2 \text{ m/s}^2$. Block $A$ has a mass of $0.5 \text{ kg}$. Determine the force exerted by block $A$ on block $B$.
questions.solution:
Let the normal force between block A and block B be $N$.

Considering the free body diagram of block A, the forces acting on it are its weight ($m_A g$) downwards and the normal reaction ($N$) upwards.

Since the elevator is accelerating downwards with an acceleration $a = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$, the equation of motion for block A is:

$m_A g - N = m_A a$

$\Rightarrow N = m_A(g - a)$

Given $m_A = 0.5 \text{ kg}$, $a = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$, and taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$N = 0.5 \times (10 - 2)$

$N = 0.5 \times 8 = 4 \text{ N}$

By Newton's third law, the force exerted by block A on block B is equal to the normal reaction $N$, which is $4 \text{ N}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a simple Atwood machine, two unequal masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are connected by a string going over a clamped light smooth pulley. In an arrangement as shown in the figure, $m_1 = 300 \text{ g}$ and $m_2 = 600 \text{ g}$. The system is released from rest. Determine the distance travelled by the first block in the first two seconds.
questions.solution:
The acceleration of the blocks in a simple Atwood machine is given by:

$a = \dfrac{m_2 - m_1}{m_1 + m_2} g$

Substituting the given values $m_1 = 300 \text{ g}$ and $m_2 = 600 \text{ g}$:

$a = \dfrac{600 - 300}{600 + 300} g = \dfrac{300}{900} g = \dfrac{g}{3}$

Taking $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$a = \dfrac{9.8}{3} \text{ m/s}^2$

The system is released from rest, so the initial velocity $u = 0$. The distance travelled by the first block in time $t = 2 \text{ s}$ is:

$s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2} a t^2$

$s = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{9.8}{3}\right) (2)^2$

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{9.8}{3} \times 4$

$s = \dfrac{19.6}{3} \approx 6.5 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a simple Atwood machine, two unequal masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are connected by a string going over a clamped light smooth pulley. In an arrangement where $m_1 = 300 \text{ g}$ and $m_2 = 600 \text{ g}$, the system is released from rest. Calculate the tension in the string.
questions.solution:
Given, $m_1 = 300 \text{ g} = 0.3 \text{ kg}$ and $m_2 = 600 \text{ g} = 0.6 \text{ kg}$.

Let $T$ be the tension in the string and $a$ be the acceleration of the masses. The equations of motion for the two masses are:
$m_2 g - T = m_2 a$
$T - m_1 g = m_1 a$

Solving these equations for tension $T$, we get:
$T = \dfrac{2 m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2} g$

Substituting the given values and $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$:
$T = \dfrac{2 \times 0.3 \times 0.6}{0.3 + 0.6} \times 9.8$

$T = \dfrac{0.36}{0.9} \times 9.8$

$T = 0.4 \times 9.8 = 3.92 \text{ N}$

Rounding to one decimal place, the tension is approximately $3.9 \text{ N}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a simple Atwood machine, two unequal masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are connected by a string going over a clamped light smooth pulley. In a setup where $m_1 = 300 \text{ g}$ and $m_2 = 600 \text{ g}$, the system is released from rest. Find the force exerted by the clamp on the pulley.
questions.solution:
Given:
$m_1 = 300 \text{ g} = 0.3 \text{ kg}$
$m_2 = 600 \text{ g} = 0.6 \text{ kg}$
Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$

The tension $T$ in the string of a simple Atwood machine is given by:
$T = \dfrac{2 m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2} g$

Substituting the given values:
$T = \dfrac{2 \times 0.3 \times 0.6}{0.3 + 0.6} \times 9.8$
$T = \dfrac{0.36}{0.9} \times 9.8$
$T = 0.4 \times 9.8 = 3.92 \text{ N}$

The pulley is light (massless), so the net force on it is zero. The upward force exerted by the clamp on the pulley must balance the total downward force acting on the pulley due to the tension in the two segments of the string.

Force exerted by the clamp $= 2T$
$F = 2 \times 3.92 \text{ N}$
$F = 7.84 \text{ N} \approx 7.8 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, a uniform rod of length $30\text{ cm}$ and mass $3.0 \text{ kg}$ is pulled by strings subjected to constant forces of $20 \text{ N}$ and $32 \text{ N}$. Determine the force exerted by the $20 \text{ cm}$ part of the rod on the $10 \text{ cm}$ part. All surfaces are smooth, and the strings and pulleys are light.
questions.solution:
Total mass of the rod, $M = 3.0 \text{ kg}$
Total length of the rod, $L = 30 \text{ cm}$

The net force acting on the rod is:
$F_{\text{net}} = 32 - 20 = 12 \text{ N}$ towards the right.

The acceleration of the rod is:
$a = \dfrac{F_{\text{net}}}{M} = \dfrac{12}{3.0} = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$

Since the rod is uniform, the mass of the $10 \text{ cm}$ part is:
$m_1 = \dfrac{10}{30} \times 3.0 = 1.0 \text{ kg}$

Let $F$ be the force exerted by the $20 \text{ cm}$ part on the $10 \text{ cm}$ part. The forces acting on the $10 \text{ cm}$ part in the horizontal direction are $20 \text{ N}$ to the left and $F$ to the right.

Applying Newton's second law to the $10 \text{ cm}$ part:
$F - 20 = m_1 a$
$F - 20 = 1.0 \times 4$
$F = 24 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the setup presented in the figure. All surfaces are frictionless, while the string and pulley are light. Determine the magnitude of the acceleration of the two blocks.
questions.solution:
The given wedge forms a triangle with sides $3\text{ m}$, $4\text{ m}$, and $5\text{ m}$. Since $3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2$, it is a right-angled triangle with the right angle at the top vertex.

Let $\theta_1$ be the angle of inclination of the left surface (length $3\text{ m}$) and $\theta_2$ be the angle of inclination of the right surface (length $4\text{ m}$).

Using the geometric properties of the triangle, the altitude $h$ to the base is given by equating the area:

$\dfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 4 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times h \Rightarrow h = \dfrac{12}{5}\text{ m}$

The sine of the angles of inclination are:

$\sin \theta_1 = \dfrac{h}{3} = \dfrac{12/5}{3} = \dfrac{4}{5}$

$\sin \theta_2 = \dfrac{h}{4} = \dfrac{12/5}{4} = \dfrac{3}{5}$

Let $a$ be the common acceleration of the two blocks and $T$ be the tension in the string. The equations of motion for the left block ($m_1 = 1.0\text{ kg}$) and the right block ($m_2 = 1.0\text{ kg}$) are:

$m_1 g \sin \theta_1 - T = m_1 a$

$T - m_2 g \sin \theta_2 = m_2 a$

Adding the two equations, we get:

$a = \dfrac{m_1 g \sin \theta_1 - m_2 g \sin \theta_2}{m_1 + m_2}$

Substituting the given values:

$a = \dfrac{1.0 \times g \times \left(\dfrac{4}{5}\right) - 1.0 \times g \times \left(\dfrac{3}{5}\right)}{1.0 + 1.0}$

$a = \dfrac{\dfrac{4g}{5} - \dfrac{3g}{5}}{2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{g}{5}}{2} = \dfrac{g}{10}$

Answer: $g/10$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a constant force $F = m_2 g/2$ is exerted on the block of mass $m_1$. The surface of the table is smooth, and the string and pulley are light. Determine the acceleration of $m_1$.
questions.solution:
Let $a$ be the acceleration of the system, assuming $m_1$ moves towards the right and $m_2$ moves downwards.
Let $T$ be the tension in the string.

The equation of motion for mass $m_1$ in the horizontal direction is:
$T - F = m_1 a$

The equation of motion for mass $m_2$ in the vertical direction is:
$m_2 g - T = m_2 a$

Adding the two equations, we get:
$m_2 g - F = (m_1 + m_2) a$

Given that the applied force is $F = \dfrac{m_2 g}{2}$, substituting this value yields:
$m_2 g - \dfrac{m_2 g}{2} = (m_1 + m_2) a$

$\dfrac{m_2 g}{2} = (m_1 + m_2) a$

Solving for $a$, we get:
$a = \dfrac{m_2 g}{2(m_1 + m_2)}$

Since the calculated acceleration $a$ is positive, our initial assumption about the direction is correct. Therefore, the acceleration of $m_1$ is $\dfrac{m_2 g}{2(m_1 + m_2)}$ towards the right.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the figure provided, the masses are $m_1 = 5 \text{ kg}$ and $m_2 = 2 \text{ kg}$, while the applied force is $F = 1 \text{ N}$. Determine the acceleration of either block. Also, describe the motion of $m_1$ if the string breaks but the force $F$ continues to act.
options: $4.0 \text{ m/s}^2$; moves downward with acceleration $g - 0.2 \text{ m/s}^2$
$4.3 \text{ m/s}^2$; moves downward with acceleration $g + 0.2 \text{ m/s}^2$
$4.6 \text{ m/s}^2$; moves downward with acceleration $g + 0.2 \text{ m/s}^2$
$4.3 \text{ m/s}^2$; moves downward with acceleration $g + 0.5 \text{ m/s}^2$
questions.solution:
Let $a$ be the common acceleration of the blocks and $T$ be the tension in the string.

For block $m_1$ (moving downward), the equation of motion is:
$m_1 g + F - T = m_1 a$

For block $m_2$ (moving upward), the equation of motion is:
$T - m_2 g - F = m_2 a$

Adding the two equations, we get:
$m_1 g - m_2 g = (m_1 + m_2) a$

$a = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1 + m_2} g$

Substituting $m_1 = 5 \text{ kg}$, $m_2 = 2 \text{ kg}$, and taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:
$a = \dfrac{5 - 2}{5 + 2} \times 10 = \dfrac{30}{7} \approx 4.3 \text{ m/s}^2$

When the string breaks, the tension $T$ becomes zero. The forces acting on $m_1$ are its weight $m_1 g$ and the applied force $F$, both directed downwards.

The net downward force on $m_1$ is:
$F_{\text{net}} = m_1 g + F$

The new acceleration $a'$ of $m_1$ is:
$a' = \dfrac{m_1 g + F}{m_1} = g + \dfrac{F}{m_1}$

Substituting $F = 1 \text{ N}$ and $m_1 = 5 \text{ kg}$:
$a' = g + \dfrac{1}{5} = g + 0.2 \text{ m/s}^2$

Thus, $m_1$ moves downward with an acceleration of $g + 0.2 \text{ m/s}^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the arrangement depicted in the figure, let $m_1 = 1 \text{ kg}$, $m_2 = 2 \text{ kg}$ and $m_3 = 3 \text{ kg}$. Determine the accelerations of the masses $m_1$, $m_2$, and $m_3$. When the system is released from rest, the string segment from the upper pulley to $m_1$ measures $20 \text{ cm}$. How much time will elapse before $m_1$ strikes the upper pulley?
questions.solution:
Let $T_1$ be the tension in the string connected to $m_1$ and $T_2$ be the tension in the string connecting $m_2$ and $m_3$. Since the lower pulley is massless, $T_1 = 2T_2$.

Let $a_1$ be the upward acceleration of $m_1$. The lower pulley will have a downward acceleration of $a_1$.
Let $a'$ be the downward acceleration of $m_3$ relative to the lower pulley. Then $m_2$ has an upward acceleration $a'$ relative to the lower pulley.
The absolute downward accelerations of $m_2$ and $m_3$ are $a_2 = a_1 - a'$ and $a_3 = a_1 + a'$.

Writing the equations of motion for each mass:
For $m_1$: $T_1 - m_1 g = m_1 a_1$
$\Rightarrow 2T_2 - g = a_1 \Rightarrow T_2 = \dfrac{a_1 + g}{2}$

For $m_2$: $m_2 g - T_2 = m_2 a_2$
$\Rightarrow 2g - T_2 = 2(a_1 - a') \Rightarrow g - \dfrac{T_2}{2} = a_1 - a'$

For $m_3$: $m_3 g - T_2 = m_3 a_3$
$\Rightarrow 3g - T_2 = 3(a_1 + a') \Rightarrow g - \dfrac{T_2}{3} = a_1 + a'$

Adding the equations for $m_2$ and $m_3$ to eliminate $a'$:
$2g - T_2\left(\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{3}\right) = 2a_1$
$2g - \dfrac{5}{6}T_2 = 2a_1$

Substituting $T_2 = \dfrac{a_1 + g}{2}$ into the above equation:
$2g - \dfrac{5}{6}\left(\dfrac{a_1 + g}{2}\right) = 2a_1$
$2g - \dfrac{5}{12}g = 2a_1 + \dfrac{5}{12}a_1$
$\dfrac{19}{12}g = \dfrac{29}{12}a_1 \Rightarrow a_1 = \dfrac{19g}{29}$ (upwards)

Now, finding $T_2$:
$T_2 = \dfrac{\dfrac{19g}{29} + g}{2} = \dfrac{24g}{29}$

Finding the accelerations $a_2$ and $a_3$:
$a_2 = g - \dfrac{T_2}{2} = g - \dfrac{12g}{29} = \dfrac{17g}{29}$ (downwards)
$a_3 = g - \dfrac{T_2}{3} = g - \dfrac{8g}{29} = \dfrac{21g}{29}$ (downwards)

To find the time elapsed before $m_1$ strikes the upper pulley, we use the kinematic equation $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$.
Given $s = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$, $u = 0$, and $a_1 = \dfrac{19g}{29}$.
$0.2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \left(\dfrac{19 \times 9.8}{29}\right) \times t^2$
$0.4 = \dfrac{186.2}{29} t^2$
$t^2 = \dfrac{0.4 \times 29}{186.2} \approx 0.0623$
$t \approx 0.25 \text{ s}$

Answer: $\dfrac{19g}{29}$ (up), $\dfrac{17g}{29}$ (down), $\dfrac{21g}{29}$ (down), $0.25 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the figure shown, suppose $m_2 = 2.0 \text{ kg}$ and $m_3 = 3.0 \text{ kg}$. What should be the mass $m_1$ so that it remains at rest?
questions.solution:
Let $T_1$ be the tension in the string connected to $m_1$, and $T_2$ be the tension in the string connecting $m_2$ and $m_3$.

For $m_1$ to remain at rest, the upward tension must balance its weight:
$T_1 = m_1 g$

Since $m_1$ is at rest, the movable pulley is also stationary. Balancing the forces on the massless movable pulley gives:
$T_1 = 2T_2$

With the movable pulley at rest, $m_2$ and $m_3$ form a simple Atwood machine. The tension $T_2$ in this lower string is:
$T_2 = \dfrac{2 m_2 m_3}{m_2 + m_3} g$

Substitute this $T_2$ back into the $T_1$ equation:
$T_1 = 2 \left( \dfrac{2 m_2 m_3}{m_2 + m_3} g \right) = \dfrac{4 m_2 m_3}{m_2 + m_3} g$

Equating the two expressions for $T_1$ gives:
$m_1 g = \dfrac{4 m_2 m_3}{m_2 + m_3} g$

$m_1 = \dfrac{4 m_2 m_3}{m_2 + m_3}$

Substitute the given values $m_2 = 2.0 \text{ kg}$ and $m_3 = 3.0 \text{ kg}$:
$m_1 = \dfrac{4(2.0)(3.0)}{2.0 + 3.0} = \dfrac{24}{5} = 4.8 \text{ kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the tension in the string as shown in the figure. The pulleys and the string are light, while all surfaces are frictionless. Use $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string and $a$ be the magnitude of acceleration of the blocks.

For the hanging block of mass $m_2 = 1 \text{ kg}$, the forces acting on it are the downward gravitational force $m_2g$ and the upward tension $T$. Assuming it accelerates downwards with acceleration $a$, its equation of motion is:
$m_2g - T = m_2a$
$10 - T = 1 \cdot a \quad \dots (1)$

For the block on the horizontal surface of mass $m_1 = 1 \text{ kg}$, the string is attached to its left side, passes around the left pulley, and then goes to the right pulley. When the hanging mass moves downwards, the string pulls the block to the left. The only horizontal force acting on this block is the tension $T$ directed to the left. Since the length of the string is constant, its acceleration is also $a$ to the left. Its equation of motion is:
$T = m_1a$
$T = 1 \cdot a \quad \dots (2)$

Substituting the value of $a$ from equation (2) into equation (1), we get:
$10 - T = T$
$2T = 10$
$T = 5 \text{ N}$

The tension in the string is $5 \text{ N}$.

Answer: $5 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the reading of the spring balance depicted in the figure. The elevator is ascending with an acceleration of $g/10$, the pulley and the string are light, and the pulley is smooth.
questions.solution:
The elevator is accelerating upwards with an acceleration $a = \dfrac{g}{10}$.

The effective acceleration due to gravity inside the elevator is:
$g_{\text{eff}} = g + a = g + \dfrac{g}{10} = \dfrac{11g}{10}$

For the masses $m_1 = 1.5 \text{ kg}$ and $m_2 = 3.0 \text{ kg}$ connected over the pulley, the tension $T$ in the string is given by:
$T = \dfrac{2m_1m_2}{m_1 + m_2} g_{\text{eff}}$

Substituting the given values:
$T = \dfrac{2 \times 1.5 \times 3.0}{1.5 + 3.0} \times \dfrac{11g}{10}$

$T = \dfrac{9.0}{4.5} \times \dfrac{11g}{10} = 2 \times \dfrac{11g}{10} = 2.2g$

The downward force exerted on the pulley, which is measured by the spring balance, is $2T$:
$F = 2T = 2 \times 2.2g = 4.4g$

Since the spring balance is calibrated in kg, its reading is the measured force divided by the true acceleration due to gravity $g$:
$\text{Reading} = \dfrac{F}{g} = \dfrac{4.4g}{g} = 4.4 \text{ kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the arrangement displayed in the figure. The pulleys and the string are light, and all surfaces are frictionless. Determine the acceleration of the mass $M$.
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string.

For the hanging mass $M$ moving downwards with acceleration $a$, the equation of motion is:
$Mg - T = Ma$

The movable pulley B is pulled towards the right by two segments of the string. The net force on the block of mass $2M$ is $2T$. Let its acceleration be $a_B$. The equation of motion is:
$2T = 2M a_B \Rightarrow T = M a_B$

Using the string constraint, if pulley B moves to the right by a distance $x$, both horizontal segments of the string shorten by $x$, releasing a total length of $2x$. Thus, mass $M$ moves downwards by $2x$.
Therefore, the acceleration of mass $M$ is twice that of mass $2M$:
$a = 2a_B \Rightarrow a_B = \dfrac{a}{2}$

Substituting $a_B$ into the tension equation:
$T = M \left(\dfrac{a}{2}\right) = \dfrac{Ma}{2}$

Substituting this value of $T$ into the first equation:
$Mg - \dfrac{Ma}{2} = Ma$

$Mg = Ma + \dfrac{Ma}{2}$

$Mg = \dfrac{3Ma}{2}$

$a = \dfrac{2g}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two speakers $S_1$ and $S_2$, powered by a common amplifier, are situated at $y = 1.0 \text{ m}$ and $y = -1.0 \text{ m}$ as shown in the figure. These speakers vibrate in phase at $600 \text{ Hz}$. A man is standing at a point on the X-axis located at a very large distance from the origin and begins to move parallel to the Y-axis. The speed of sound in air is given as $330 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. If he continues walking along the same line past the first maximum, how many additional maxima can he hear?
questions.solution:
The wavelength of the sound wave is given by:

$\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f} = \dfrac{330}{600} = 0.55 \text{ m}$

The distance between the two speakers $S_1$ and $S_2$ is:

$d = 1.0 - (-1.0) = 2.0 \text{ m}$

For an observer at a very large distance from the origin, the path difference $\Delta x$ at an angle $\theta$ from the X-axis is:

$\Delta x = d \sin \theta$

For a maximum (constructive interference), the path difference must be an integer multiple of the wavelength:

$\Delta x = n\lambda \implies d \sin \theta = n\lambda$

Substituting the values, we get:

$2.0 \sin \theta = n(0.55) \implies \sin \theta = 0.275 n$

Since the maximum value of $\sin \theta$ is $1$ (as $\theta \to 90^\circ$ when the man moves to infinity parallel to the Y-axis):

$0.275 n \le 1 \implies n \le \dfrac{1}{0.275} \approx 3.63$

The possible non-negative integer values for $n$ are $0, 1, 2, 3$.

The man starts at a point on the X-axis, where $\theta = 0^\circ$. This corresponds to the central maximum ($n = 0$).

As he moves parallel to the Y-axis, $\theta$ increases, and he will successively encounter the maxima for $n = 1, 2, 3$.

The first maximum he encounters while walking corresponds to $n = 1$.

If he continues walking past this first maximum, the additional maxima he will hear correspond to $n = 2$ and $n = 3$.

Therefore, he will hear $2$ additional maxima.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the acceleration of the block of mass $M$ for the setup shown in the figure. Assume that all surfaces are frictionless and the pulleys and string are light.
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string. The block of mass $M$ is pulled up the incline by a single string, so the tension force acting on it is $T$. The movable pulley is supported by two segments of the same string, so the total upward force on it is $2T$.

Let the downward acceleration of the block of mass $2M$ be $a$. By string constraint, if the movable pulley moves downwards by a distance $x$, it requires $2x$ length of string. This string comes from the portion on the incline, meaning the block of mass $M$ must move up the incline by a distance $2x$. Therefore, the acceleration of the block of mass $M$ up the incline is $2a$.

Writing the equation of motion for the block of mass $M$ along the incline:
$T - Mg \sin(30^{\circ}) = M(2a)$
$T - \dfrac{Mg}{2} = 2Ma$

Writing the equation of motion for the block of mass $2M$ moving downwards:
$2Mg - 2T = (2M)a$
$Mg - T = Ma$

Adding the two equations:
$\left(T - \dfrac{Mg}{2}\right) + (Mg - T) = 2Ma + Ma$
$\dfrac{Mg}{2} = 3Ma$
$a = \dfrac{g}{6}$

The acceleration of the block of mass $M$ is $2a$ up the incline:
$2a = 2 \left(\dfrac{g}{6}\right) = \dfrac{g}{3}$

Thus, the acceleration of the block of mass $M$ is $\dfrac{g}{3}$ up the plane.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two speakers $S_1$ and $S_2$, powered by a common amplifier, are situated at $y = 1.0 \text{ m}$ and $y = -1.0 \text{ m}$ as shown in the figure. These speakers vibrate in phase at $600 \text{ Hz}$. A man is standing at a point on the X-axis located at a very large distance from the origin and begins to move parallel to the Y-axis. The speed of sound in air is given as $330 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. At what angle $\theta$ will the man experience a maximum of sound intensity for the first time?
questions.solution:
The distance between the two speakers $S_1$ and $S_2$ is $d = 1.0 - (-1.0) = 2.0 \text{ m}$.

The wavelength of the sound wave is given by:

$\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f} = \dfrac{330}{600} = 0.55 \text{ m}$

For a point at a very large distance from the origin at an angle $\theta$ with the X-axis, the path difference between the sound waves reaching the man from $S_1$ and $S_2$ is:

$\Delta x = d \sin \theta$

The man starts from the X-axis ($\theta = 0^\circ$), where there is a central maximum. As he moves parallel to the Y-axis, he will experience the first maximum of sound intensity when the path difference equals one wavelength ($\lambda$).

$\Delta x = \lambda$

$d \sin \theta = \lambda$

Substituting the values, we get:

$2.0 \sin \theta = 0.55$

$\sin \theta = \dfrac{0.55}{2.0} = 0.275$

We know that $\sin 15^\circ = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}-1}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.259$. Since $0.275$ is slightly greater than $0.259$, the angle $\theta$ must be slightly greater than $15^\circ$.

From the given options, the closest angle is $16^\circ$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a simple Atwood machine, two unequal masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are connected by a string going over a clamped light smooth pulley. In a typical arrangement shown in the figure, $m_1 = 300 \text{ g}$ and $m_2 = 600 \text{ g}$. The system is released from rest. The larger mass is stopped for a moment $2.0 \text{ s}$ after the system is set into motion. Find the time elapsed before the string is tight again.
questions.solution:
Given $m_1 = 300 \text{ g}$ and $m_2 = 600 \text{ g}$.

The initial acceleration of the system is:
$a = \dfrac{m_2 - m_1}{m_2 + m_1} g = \dfrac{600 - 300}{600 + 300} g = \dfrac{300}{900} g = \dfrac{g}{3}$

The velocity of the system just before the larger mass is stopped ($t = 2.0 \text{ s}$) is:
$v = u + at = 0 + \left(\dfrac{g}{3}\right)(2) = \dfrac{2g}{3}$

When $m_2$ is momentarily stopped, its velocity becomes zero. However, $m_1$ is still moving upwards with velocity $v = \dfrac{2g}{3}$. At this point, the string becomes slack.

Both masses now move freely under gravity. Let $t$ be the time until the string becomes tight again.

For the string to become tight, the distance $m_1$ moves relative to the pulley must equal the distance $m_2$ falls. In other words, the upward displacement of $m_1$ must equal the downward displacement of $m_2$.

Displacement of $m_1$ (moving upwards against gravity):
$y_1 = vt - \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$

Displacement of $m_2$ (falling downwards from rest):
$y_2 = \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$

Equating the displacements for the string to regain tension:
$vt - \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$

$vt = gt^2$

Since $t \neq 0$, we have:
$t = \dfrac{v}{g}$

Substitute $v = \dfrac{2g}{3}$:
$t = \dfrac{\dfrac{2g}{3}}{g} = \dfrac{2}{3} \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the mass $M$ of the hanging block shown in the figure that will prevent the smaller block from slipping over the triangular block. Assume all surfaces are frictionless and that the string and pulley are light.
questions.solution:
Let the acceleration of the system be $a$ to the right. For the smaller block of mass $m$ to not slip over the triangular block, it must move with the same horizontal acceleration $a$ as the triangular block.

Considering the free body diagram of the smaller block $m$ from the ground frame, the forces acting on it are its weight $mg$ downwards and the normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to the inclined surface.

Resolving the normal reaction $N$ into horizontal and vertical components:
Vertical equilibrium: $N \cos \theta = mg$
Horizontal acceleration: $N \sin \theta = ma$

Dividing the two equations, we get the required acceleration for no slipping:
$\tan \theta = \dfrac{a}{g} \implies a = g \tan \theta$

Now, considering the entire system of masses $(M' + m)$ moving horizontally and $M$ moving vertically downwards, the common acceleration $a$ is given by the net pulling force divided by the total mass:
$a = \dfrac{Mg}{M + M' + m}$

Equating the two expressions for $a$:
$\dfrac{Mg}{M + M' + m} = g \tan \theta$

Canceling $g$ and rearranging to solve for $M$:
$M = (M + M' + m) \tan \theta$
$M - M \tan \theta = (M' + m) \tan \theta$
$M(1 - \tan \theta) = (M' + m) \tan \theta$

$M = \dfrac{(M' + m) \tan \theta}{1 - \tan \theta}$

Dividing the numerator and the denominator by $\tan \theta$:
$M = \dfrac{M' + m}{\cot \theta - 1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the accelerations of the blocks $A$ and $B$ for the three situations depicted in the figure.
options:
(a) $A$: $\dfrac{g}{7}$ downward, $B$: $\dfrac{2g}{7}$ upward
(b) $A$: $\dfrac{5g}{13}$ right, $B$: $\dfrac{10g}{13}$ downward
(c) $A$: $\dfrac{g}{3}$ downward, $B$: $\dfrac{2g}{3}$ upward

(a) $A$: $\dfrac{2g}{7}$ downward, $B$: $\dfrac{g}{7}$ upward
(b) $A$: $\dfrac{10g}{13}$ right, $B$: $\dfrac{5g}{13}$ downward
(c) $A$: $\dfrac{2g}{3}$ downward, $B$: $\dfrac{g}{3}$ upward

(a) $A$: $\dfrac{g}{7}$ upward, $B$: $\dfrac{2g}{7}$ downward
(b) $A$: $\dfrac{5g}{13}$ left, $B$: $\dfrac{10g}{13}$ upward
(c) $A$: $\dfrac{g}{3}$ upward, $B$: $\dfrac{2g}{3}$ downward

(a) $A$: $\dfrac{2g}{7}$ upward, $B$: $\dfrac{g}{7}$ downward
(b) $A$: $\dfrac{10g}{13}$ left, $B$: $\dfrac{5g}{13}$ upward
(c) $A$: $\dfrac{2g}{3}$ upward, $B$: $\dfrac{g}{3}$ downward
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string for each case.

For situation (a):
Let the downward acceleration of block $A$ be $a$. Since the string is continuous, if block $A$ moves down by a distance $x$, the movable pulley (and block $B$) must move up by a distance $x/2$. Thus, the upward acceleration of block $B$ is $a/2$.
The equations of motion are:
For block $A$ ($4\text{ kg}$): $4g - T = 4a$
For block $B$ ($5\text{ kg}$): $2T - 5g = 5\left(\dfrac{a}{2}\right)$
Multiplying the first equation by $2$ gives $8g - 2T = 8a$.
Adding this to the second equation:
$8g - 5g = 8a + \dfrac{5a}{2}$
$3g = \dfrac{21a}{2} \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{6g}{21} = \dfrac{2g}{7}$
Thus, the acceleration of $A$ is $\dfrac{2g}{7}$ downward, and the acceleration of $B$ is $\dfrac{g}{7}$ upward.

For situation (b):
Let the rightward acceleration of block $A$ be $a$. If block $A$ moves right by $x$, the movable pulley moves down by $x/2$. Thus, the downward acceleration of block $B$ is $a/2$.
The equations of motion are:
For block $A$ ($2\text{ kg}$): $T = 2a$
For block $B$ ($5\text{ kg}$): $5g - 2T = 5\left(\dfrac{a}{2}\right)$
Substituting $T = 2a$ into the second equation:
$5g - 2(2a) = \dfrac{5a}{2}$
$5g - 4a = \dfrac{5a}{2} \Rightarrow 5g = \dfrac{13a}{2} \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{10g}{13}$
Thus, the acceleration of $A$ is $\dfrac{10g}{13}$ right, and the acceleration of $B$ is $\dfrac{5g}{13}$ downward.

For situation (c):
Let the downward acceleration of block $A$ be $a$. If block $A$ moves down by $x$, the movable pulley moves up by $x/2$. Thus, the upward acceleration of block $B$ is $a/2$.
The equations of motion are:
For block $A$ ($2\text{ kg}$): $2g - T = 2a$
For block $B$ ($1\text{ kg}$): $2T - 1g = 1\left(\dfrac{a}{2}\right)$
Multiplying the first equation by $2$ gives $4g - 2T = 4a$.
Adding this to the second equation:
$4g - g = 4a + \dfrac{a}{2}$
$3g = \dfrac{9a}{2} \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{6g}{9} = \dfrac{2g}{3}$
Thus, the acceleration of $A$ is $\dfrac{2g}{3}$ downward, and the acceleration of $B$ is $\dfrac{g}{3}$ upward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In addition to the force of gravity, a force $\vec{F} = \vec{v} \times \vec{A}$ acts on a particle, where $\vec{v}$ represents the particle's velocity and $\vec{A}$ is a constant horizontal vector. Determine the minimum speed at which a particle of mass $m$ must be projected to ensure it travels undeflected at a constant velocity.
questions.solution:
For the particle to move undeflected at a constant velocity, the net force acting on it must be zero.

The forces acting on the particle are the gravitational force $m\vec{g}$ and the given force $\vec{F} = \vec{v} \times \vec{A}$.

Equating the net force to zero gives:

$m\vec{g} + \vec{v} \times \vec{A} = 0$

$\vec{v} \times \vec{A} = -m\vec{g}$

Taking the magnitude on both sides yields:

$v A \sin \theta = mg$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ and the constant horizontal vector $\vec{A}$.

To minimize the speed $v$, $\sin \theta$ must be maximized. The maximum value of $\sin \theta$ is $1$, which occurs when $\theta = 90^{\circ}$.

Substituting $\sin \theta = 1$ gives:

$v_{min} A = mg$

$v_{min} = \dfrac{mg}{A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, monkey B is holding on to the tail of monkey A, which is climbing up a rope. The masses of monkeys A and B are $5 \text{ kg}$ and $2 \text{ kg}$, respectively. Given that A can tolerate a tension of $30 \text{ N}$ in its tail, what force should it apply on the rope to carry monkey B with it? Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let $a$ be the upward acceleration of the monkeys.

For monkey B, the equation of motion is:

$T - m_B g = m_B a$

Given $m_B = 2 \text{ kg}$ and $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$T - 20 = 2a \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{T - 20}{2}$

For the system of monkeys A and B, the equation of motion is:

$F - (m_A + m_B)g = (m_A + m_B)a$

Given $m_A = 5 \text{ kg}$:

$F - 70 = 7a$

Substituting the value of $a$:

$F = 70 + 7 \left( \dfrac{T - 20}{2} \right) = 70 + 3.5(T - 20)$

To carry monkey B, the minimum upward acceleration is $a = 0$, which gives the minimum tension $T = 20 \text{ N}$.

The maximum tension monkey A's tail can tolerate is $T = 30 \text{ N}$.

For $T = 20 \text{ N}$, $F_{min} = 70 + 3.5(20 - 20) = 70 \text{ N}$.

For $T = 30 \text{ N}$, $F_{max} = 70 + 3.5(30 - 20) = 105 \text{ N}$.

Therefore, the force applied by monkey A should be between $70 \text{ N}$ and $105 \text{ N}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, a man of mass $60 \text{ kg}$ stands on a light weighing machine placed inside a box of mass $30 \text{ kg}$. The box is suspended from a pulley fixed to the ceiling via a light rope, while the man grasps the rope's other end. Determine the weight indicated by the machine if the man keeps the box stationary. Furthermore, determine the force he must exert on the rope so that the machine displays his correct weight.
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the man be $M = 60 \text{ kg}$ and the mass of the box be $m = 30 \text{ kg}$. Let $T$ be the tension in the rope and $N$ be the normal reaction between the man and the weighing machine.

When the box is stationary, the acceleration of the system is zero. Considering the man and the box as a single system, the upward forces are the tensions from the two segments of the rope ($2T$), and the downward force is the total weight.

$2T = (M + m)g$

$2T = (60 + 30)g = 90g \Rightarrow T = 45g$

Considering the forces acting on the man alone, the upward forces are the normal reaction $N$ and the tension $T$, while the downward force is his weight $Mg$.

$N + T = Mg$

$N + 45g = 60g \Rightarrow N = 15g$

The weight indicated by the weighing machine is $\dfrac{N}{g} = 15 \text{ kg}$.

When the machine displays the man's correct weight, the normal reaction is equal to the true weight of the man, so $N = 60g$. Let the system accelerate upwards with an acceleration $a$.

Applying Newton's second law for the man:

$N + T - Mg = Ma$

$60g + T - 60g = 60a \Rightarrow T = 60a \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{T}{60}$

Applying Newton's second law for the entire system:

$2T - (M + m)g = (M + m)a$

$2T - 90g = 90a$

Substituting $a = \dfrac{T}{60}$:

$2T - 90g = 90\left(\dfrac{T}{60}\right)$

$2T - 90g = 1.5T$

$0.5T = 90g \Rightarrow T = 180g$

Taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, the force the man must exert on the rope is:

$T = 180 \times 10 = 1800 \text{ N}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block $A$ can slide on a frictionless incline of angle $\theta$ and length $l$, kept inside an elevator travelling up with uniform velocity $v$ as shown in the figure. Determine the time taken by the block to slide down the length of the incline if it is released from the top.
questions.solution:
Since the elevator is moving upwards with a uniform velocity $v$, its acceleration is zero. Therefore, there is no pseudo force acting on the block, and the effective acceleration due to gravity inside the elevator remains $g$.

The component of acceleration of the block along the frictionless incline is $a = g \sin \theta$.

The block is released from rest, so its initial velocity is $u = 0$. Let $t$ be the time taken to slide down the incline of length $l$.

Using the second equation of motion, $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$:

$l = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}(g \sin \theta)t^2$

$t^2 = \dfrac{2l}{g \sin \theta}$

$t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2l}{g \sin \theta}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car accelerates on a horizontal road with an acceleration $a$. Two situations are considered inside the car. First, a ball is suspended from the ceiling by a string and maintains a constant angle with the vertical. Second, a block is placed on a smooth incline and does not slip on the incline. Determine the angle of the string with the vertical, and the angle of the incline with the horizontal.
questions.solution:
In the frame of the accelerating car, a pseudo force $ma$ acts on the objects in the direction opposite to the acceleration.

For the suspended ball, the forces acting are tension $T$, weight $mg$, and pseudo force $ma$.

Resolving forces along the horizontal and vertical directions for equilibrium:

$T \sin \theta = ma$

$T \cos \theta = mg$

Dividing the two equations, we get $\tan \theta = \dfrac{a}{g} \Rightarrow \theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{a}{g}\right)$.

For the block on the smooth incline, the forces acting are normal reaction $N$, weight $mg$, and pseudo force $ma$.

For the block to not slip, the net force along the incline must be zero.

The component of gravity down the incline is $mg \sin \alpha$ and the component of the pseudo force up the incline is $ma \cos \alpha$.

Equating these components:

$mg \sin \alpha = ma \cos \alpha$

$\tan \alpha = \dfrac{a}{g} \Rightarrow \alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{a}{g}\right)$.

Both angles are $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{a}{g}\right)$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block rests on the floor of a stationary elevator. The elevator then begins to descend with an acceleration of $12 \text{ m/s}^2$. Determine the displacement of the block during the first $0.2 \text{ s}$ after the start. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
The downward acceleration of the elevator is $a = 12 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Since the downward acceleration of the elevator is greater than the acceleration due to gravity ($a > g$), the normal force becomes zero and the block loses contact with the floor of the elevator.

The block will undergo free fall under gravity with an acceleration $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Using the second equation of motion for the block:

$s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$

Given the initial velocity $u = 0$ and time $t = 0.2 \text{ s}$:

$s = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (0.2)^2$

$s = 5 \times 0.04 = 0.2 \text{ m}$

$s = 20 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man has fallen into a ditch of width $d$, and two of his friends are slowly pulling him out using a light rope and two fixed pulleys as shown in the figure. Assuming the force exerted by both friends is equal, find the force applied by each friend on the rope when the man is at a depth $h$.
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the force applied by each friend. Since the man is pulled up slowly, he is in equilibrium.

The horizontal components of the tension forces balance each other, which implies the man is exactly in the middle of the ditch. Thus, the horizontal distance from the man to each pulley is $\dfrac{d}{2}$.

Let $\theta$ be the angle each rope makes with the vertical. From the geometry of the system, the length of the rope from the pulley to the man is given by:

$L = \sqrt{h^2 + \left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{4h^2 + d^2}}{2}$

The cosine of the angle $\theta$ is:

$\cos\theta = \dfrac{h}{L} = \dfrac{2h}{\sqrt{d^2 + 4h^2}}$

Balancing the forces in the vertical direction, the upward components of the tension from both ropes must equal the weight of the man:

$2T \cos\theta = mg$

Substituting the value of $\cos\theta$ into the equation:

$2T \left( \dfrac{2h}{\sqrt{d^2 + 4h^2}} \right) = mg$

$\dfrac{4Th}{\sqrt{d^2 + 4h^2}} = mg$

Solving for $T$, we get the force applied by each friend:

$T = \dfrac{mg\sqrt{d^2 + 4h^2}}{4h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A monkey is climbing on a rope that passes over a smooth light pulley and supports a block of equal mass at the other end, as shown in the figure. Assume that initially both were at rest. Irrespective of the force the monkey exerts on the rope, which of the following correctly describes the motion of the monkey and the block?
options: The monkey and the block move in the same direction with equal acceleration, and their separation will not change as time passes.
The block moves with a greater acceleration than the monkey, and their separation will increase as time passes.
The monkey moves with a greater acceleration than the block, and their separation will decrease as time passes.
The monkey and the block move in opposite directions with equal acceleration, and their separation will increase as time passes.
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the monkey and the block be $m$.

Let $T$ be the tension in the rope. Since the pulley is smooth and light, the tension remains the same on both sides of the rope.

The forces acting on the block are the tension $T$ upwards and its weight $mg$ downwards. The equation of motion for the block is:

$T - mg = m a_b$

where $a_b$ is the upward acceleration of the block.

The forces acting on the monkey are the tension $T$ upwards (since the force the monkey exerts on the rope equals the tension) and its weight $mg$ downwards. The equation of motion for the monkey is:

$T - mg = m a_m$

where $a_m$ is the upward acceleration of the monkey.

From the two equations, we get:

$m a_b = m a_m \Rightarrow a_b = a_m$

Since both the monkey and the block start from rest and have the same acceleration, their velocities and displacements will be equal at all times. Therefore, they will move in the same direction with equal acceleration, and the distance between them will remain constant as time passes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the acceleration of the $500\text{ g}$ block as shown in the figure.
questions.solution:
Let $a$ be the common acceleration of the system and $T_1, T_2$ be the tensions in the strings.

Assuming the $500\text{ g}$ block moves downward, the equations of motion for the three masses are:

For the $500\text{ g}$ block:
$500g - T_2 = 500a$

For the $100\text{ g}$ block on the incline:
$T_2 - T_1 - 100g \sin 30^\circ = 100a$

For the $50\text{ g}$ block:
$T_1 - 50g = 50a$

Adding the three equations, we get:
$(500g - T_2) + (T_2 - T_1 - 100g \sin 30^\circ) + (T_1 - 50g) = (500 + 100 + 50)a$

$500g - 100g \times \dfrac{1}{2} - 50g = 650a$

$500g - 50g - 50g = 650a$

$400g = 650a$

$a = \dfrac{400g}{650} = \dfrac{8g}{13}$

Since $a$ is positive, the $500\text{ g}$ block moves downward with an acceleration of $\dfrac{8g}{13}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A monkey of mass $15 \text{ kg}$ climbs a rope having one end fixed to the ceiling. What force should it apply to the rope to ascend with an acceleration of $1 \text{ m/s}^2$? Furthermore, if the rope is $5 \text{ m}$ long and the monkey starts from rest, how much time will it take to reach the ceiling? (Assume $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the rope, which is equal to the force applied by the monkey.

The equation of motion for the monkey moving upwards with acceleration $a$ is given by

$T - mg = ma$

$T = m(g + a)$

Substituting $m = 15 \text{ kg}$, $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $a = 1 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$T = 15(10 + 1) = 165 \text{ N}$

To find the time taken to reach the ceiling, use the kinematic equation:

$s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$

Given $s = 5 \text{ m}$, $u = 0$, and $a = 1 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$5 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}(1)t^2$

$t^2 = 10$

$t = \sqrt{10} \text{ s}$
